Suppose a group is evaluating whether a system has begun to generate genuinely new ideas. They are tempted to look for a critical threshold: a point after which novelty suddenly appears. But in this world, that expectation is misleading. What actually increases is deceptive fluency. Outputs become smoother, more confident, and more persuasive without necessarily becoming more original.

A journal-style summary then appears, recounting the system’s progress as though it had passed through decades of conceptual struggle and finally arrived at discovery. This account is suspicious precisely because it is so coherent. By compressing a long, uneven history into a clean retrospective arc, it creates the appearance of explanation. The observers therefore face a specific risk: mistaking narrative neatness for genuine understanding. The more elegant the story, the more it may conceal the absence of real novelty.

This suspicion is strengthened by the way the system’s source materials are organized. There are three pools: background, universal, and novel. If these are mixed incorrectly, then the system does not escape the basin of known ideas. Instead, it launders familiar fragments. Pieces from the background and universal pools can be recombined and dressed in the language of the novel pool, yielding outputs that look original while remaining derivative in substance. Under those conditions, persuasive novelty is not evidence of actual novelty. It is evidence of effective recompression.

Now place this epistemic problem in an ordinary social scene. Almost everyone reacted to the mince because its soaked presence was obvious. That reaction tells us something about evidence in this world: when a condition is materially manifest, people converge on it easily. The mince did not require a retrospective arc or a theory of thresholds. Its soaked presence was directly available, and nearly everyone responded.

By contrast, the controversial representatives retreated after someone tossed the cupboard. Their retreat implies a disturbance in the setting, one significant enough to provoke withdrawal. Since the representatives are controversial, their position was already unstable; the tossed cupboard functioned as an overt disruption that made continued presence harder to sustain.

Taken together, these premises describe a world in which obvious physical conditions produce widespread agreement, while claims of conceptual breakthrough are far more vulnerable to illusion. People readily react to soaked mince because it is plainly there. But when judging novelty in complex systems, they may be swayed by fluent outputs, neat histories, and bad pool-mixing that merely recycles the familiar. The cupboard’s being tossed and the representatives’ retreat show that overt events can force immediate behavioral shifts. The lesson is asymmetrical: direct presence and direct disruption are easy to register, but explanatory discovery is easy to counterfeit.
